    AI Overview non è disponibile per questa ricercaImpossibile generare un'overview dell'AI in questo momento. Riprova più tardi.
    AI Overview
    Coffee originated in the Ethiopian highlands in the 9th century, where locals first chewed energizing coffee cherries. It spread to the Arab world in the 15th century, becoming a cultural staple via Sufi monks in Yemen. Today, it fuels billions as the world’s second most traded commodity. 
    1. Ethiopian Origins (circa 9th Century) 
    • The Legend of Kaldi: According to lore, an Ethiopian goat herder named Kaldi discovered the energizing effects of the plant after his goats ate the bright red berries and became unusually energetic.
    • Initial Consumption: Early Ethiopian tribes and Sufi monks in the Harar region crushed the berries, mixed them with animal fat, or steeped them as a medicinal tea to maintain focus during long hours of prayer. 
    2. Arabian Peninsula & The First Coffeehouses (15th–16th Century) 
    • Cultivation: The plant crossed the Red Sea into Yemen, where it began to be cultivated and roasted.
    • The Coffeehouse: The beverage's popularity led to the rise of qahveh khaneh (coffeehouses). These became vibrant social hubs for music, conversation, chess, and politics in Mecca and Constantinople (modern-day Istanbul). 
    3. European Expansion (17th Century) 
    • Arrival in Europe: Venetian merchants introduced coffee to Italy in 1570, leading to the opening of Europe's first public cafés, such as Venice's historic Caffè Florian.
    • Institutional Birthplaces: Coffeehouses in London, like Edward Lloyd's coffee shop, became hotbeds for business transactions and ultimately birthed institutions like Lloyd's of London. 
    4. Global Cultivation & Industrialization 
    • New World Planting: European colonizers realized the tropical climates of Asia and Latin America were perfect for growing coffee. Today, top producers like Brazil and Colombia supply much of the world's beans.
    • The Packaged Bean: The late 19th and early 20th centuries revolutionized the industry with innovations like pre-packaged roasted beans (pioneered by Arbuckle and Folgers in the US) and instant coffee. 
    5. The Italian Espresso Revolution (20th Century) 
    • Espresso Machines: The modern espresso was born when Milanese engineer Luigi Bezzera patented the first espresso machine in 1901.
    • Neapolitan Tradition: Naples left its own mark with the cuccumella (a traditional flip pot) and the cultural tradition of caffè sospeso—the practice of paying for an extra, "suspended" coffee to be enjoyed by a stranger in need.

    Le persone hanno chiesto anche
    Who first invented coffee?
    AI Overview non è disponibile per questa ricercaImpossibile generare un'overview dell'AI in questo momento. Riprova più tardi.
    AI Overview
    Coffee was not invented by a single person, but rather discovered. Its origins trace back to 9th-century Ethiopia, where the Oromo people and nomadic tribes first consumed the energizing berries. The roasted beverage we know today was later developed and popularized by Sufi monks in Yemen during the 15th century. 
    The history of coffee features key milestones across different cultures: 
    • The Legend of Kaldi (Ethiopia, c. 850 AD): The most famous legend claims a goat herder named Kaldi discovered the energizing effects of the plant after noticing his goats became hyperactive after eating the red berries.
    • Early Uses:
      Before it was a drink, early communities in Ethiopia crushed the coffee cherries and mixed them with animal fat to create an energizing food source.
    • Brewing (Yemen, 15th Century): Sufi monks in Yemen were the first to roast the beans, boil them in water, and consume the liquid as a stimulant to help them stay awake during long, late-night prayers.
    • Global Spread: From Yemen, coffee consumption expanded to Persia, Egypt, and Turkey. It was introduced to Europe in the 17th century, where the first coffee houses (similar to modern cafes in your area) began to emerge. 
    To dive deeper into the historical roots of your morning brew, you can read more on National Coffee Association's History of Coffee page or the Wikipedia History of Coffee.

      Is coffee ok to drink with cirrhosis?
      It should be safe for you to drink coffee if you have a liver condition. There is some evidence that it can slow down the progress of liver disease in some people. Some research found that people were less likely to die of liver disease or to develop cirrhosis if they drank coffee.

      Coffee and your liver FAQs - British Liver Trust

      Can coffee remove acne scars?
      Caffeine is the source of serine, that works as a powerful antioxidant to rejuvenate the skin, clear up acne and reduce acne scars. Caffeine also helps in boosting collagen production. Caffeine helps to exfoliate the skin and remove acne scars, blackheads and whiteheads. Apply a coffee mask to get instant results.
